# Python 3.11+
"""Domain model for the TTL key-value store.

The "nouns" of the system live here: the typed errors, the value the caller
stores, and the Entry that wraps a value with its expiry. Everything in this
file is plain single-threaded logic — there is not one lock in here. The
interesting design choice is that the Entry knows how to answer "am I expired?"
all by itself; nothing else in the system re-derives expiry.
"""

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Any


# ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# One base error for the whole store, then one specific subclass per failure
# mode. Catching TTLStoreError catches everything we raise; catching the
# subclass pinpoints exactly which rule was broken.
# 🎙️ "I define one base exception for the store and a specific subclass per
#     failure. Callers can catch the base to mean 'any store error' or the
#     specific one to react to a single failure mode. I never raise a bare
#     ValueError or RuntimeError from my own code."
class TTLStoreError(Exception):
    """Base class for every error this store raises."""


class InvalidKeyError(TTLStoreError):
    """Raised when a key is not a non-empty string."""


class InvalidTTLError(TTLStoreError):
    """Raised when a ttl is not a positive number of seconds."""


# ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# An Entry is the value PLUS the exact instant it stops being valid. It is the
# Information Expert for expiry: it holds the expires_at it computed, so it is
# the right object to answer "are you expired at time T?". If expiry logic lived
# in the store instead, every read path would have to recompute it and one of
# them would eventually get the comparison wrong.
# 🎙️ "Each Entry owns its own expiry deadline and the one method that checks it.
#     The store never recomputes expiry — it just asks the Entry. That keeps the
#     'is it dead yet' rule in exactly one place."
@dataclass
class Entry:
    """A stored value together with the absolute time it expires."""

    value: Any
    # WHY: store the ABSOLUTE deadline (now + ttl), not the ttl itself. If we
    #      kept the ttl we would also need to keep the put-time and re-add them
    #      on every read — two fields and an addition instead of one comparison.
    expires_at: float

    def is_expired(self, now: float) -> bool:
        # WHY: ">=" not ">". An entry put with ttl=5 at t=0 dies AT t=5, not
        #      after t=5. The instant the deadline arrives the value is gone —
        #      this is the off-by-one the interviewer always probes.
        return now >= self.expires_at

# Python 3.11+
"""The Clock seam.

Anything that depends on "what time is it now" goes through a Clock interface
instead of calling time.monotonic() directly. In production we wire the real
clock; in tests we wire a fake one we can move forward by hand. That is the
whole reason expiry is testable without a single time.sleep().
"""

import time
from typing import Protocol


# ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Clock is the time seam. The store never reads the wall clock directly — it
# asks whatever Clock it was handed. Swap the Clock and you control time.
# 🎙️ "I pass the clock in as a dependency. Core code calls clock.now() instead
#     of the real time function, so in a test I hand it a fake clock and just
#     move it forward — no sleeping, tests run in milliseconds."
class Clock(Protocol):
    """Returns the current time as a float number of seconds."""

    def now(self) -> float:
        ...


class SystemClock:
    """The real clock — monotonic so it never jumps backward on NTP sync."""

    def now(self) -> float:
        # WHY: monotonic, not time.time(). A TTL must not be affected by the
        #      system clock being corrected backwards; monotonic only ever
        #      increases, which is exactly what an elapsed-time deadline needs.
        return time.monotonic()


class FakeClock:
    """A clock the tests drive by hand — starts at 0, advances on command."""

    def __init__(self, start: float = 0.0) -> None:
        self._t = start

    def now(self) -> float:
        return self._t

    def advance(self, seconds: float) -> None:
        # WHY: this is the "pretend it is N seconds later" lever. No sleeping,
        #      so a 7-day-TTL test runs as fast as a 1-second one.
        self._t += seconds

# Python 3.11+
"""The orchestrator: a TTL key-value store.

This file holds TWO versions of the store:

  1. TTLStore           — the CLEAN, single-threaded core. Zero locks. The one
                          concurrency seam is a do-nothing `_lock()` context
                          manager that just yields. Read it top to bottom and
                          it reads like ordinary single-threaded code.

  2. ThreadSafeTTLStore — a subclass that overrides ONLY `_lock()` to take a
                          real reentrant lock, and adds a background sweeper
                          thread. The core logic is inherited untouched.

The interesting part of THIS problem is expiry runs on TWO clocks at once:
LAZY expiry on read (get() drops a key it finds expired) AND an ACTIVE sweeper
thread that periodically reaps expired keys so dead entries do not pile up
between reads. Both must agree, and neither may double-free or miscount.
"""

import threading
from collections.abc import Iterator
from contextlib import contextmanager

from gateways import Clock, SystemClock
from models import Entry, InvalidKeyError, InvalidTTLError


# ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# TTLStore is the brain. It owns the one dict mapping key -> Entry and the
# injected clock. Every public call flows through here. The expiry RULE lives
# on the Entry; the store just enforces WHEN to apply it (on read, on count,
# and when the sweeper asks).
# 🎙️ "The store owns one map from key to Entry plus an injected clock. It never
#     reads the wall clock itself and it never recomputes expiry — it asks the
#     Entry. This clean version has no locks at all; the thread-safe version is
#     a 15-line subclass."
class TTLStore:
    """In-memory key-value store where every key has a time-to-live."""

    def __init__(self, clock: Clock | None = None) -> None:
        # WHY: default to the real clock so production callers write
        #      TTLStore(); tests pass a FakeClock to control expiry.
        self._clock: Clock = clock or SystemClock()
        # WHY: a plain dict gives O(1) put/get/delete by key, which is exactly
        #      the operation set. A sweep is O(n) but runs off the hot path.
        self._entries: dict[str, Entry] = {}

    # ── the concurrency seam (Template Method) ──────────────────────────────
    # WHY: this does NOTHING in the clean version — it just yields. The whole
    #      file below is written as if single-threaded. The thread-safe
    #      subclass overrides this one method with a real RLock. That is the
    #      entire diff between "clean" and "thread-safe".
    @contextmanager
    def _lock(self) -> Iterator[None]:
        yield

    # ── public surface ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    def put(self, key: str, value: object, ttl: float) -> None:
        """Store value under key, alive for ttl seconds from now."""
        self._validate_key(key)
        self._validate_ttl(ttl)

        # WHY: snapshot the clock ONCE, before the lock, so the deadline is the
        #      same regardless of how long we wait for the lock. expires_at is
        #      absolute (now + ttl), computed here so the Entry stays dumb data.
        expires_at = self._clock.now() + ttl

        with self._lock():
            # WHY: a put for an existing key REPLACES it — new value, fresh TTL.
            #      Assigning over the key is exactly that; no special-casing.
            self._entries[key] = Entry(value=value, expires_at=expires_at)

    def get(self, key: str) -> object | None:
        """Return the live value for key, or None if missing or expired."""
        self._validate_key(key)
        now = self._clock.now()

        with self._lock():
            entry = self._entries.get(key)

            if entry is None:
                # WHY: a missing key returns None — get is a lookup, not an
                #      assertion. Callers that want "must exist" check for None.
                return None

            if entry.is_expired(now):
                # WHY: LAZY expiry. We found a dead key on the read path, so we
                #      reclaim it right now instead of waiting for the sweeper.
                #      pop(key, None) is safe even if a racing sweeper already
                #      removed it — the second remove is a no-op, never an error.
                self._entries.pop(key, None)
                return None

            return entry.value

    def delete(self, key: str) -> bool:
        """Remove key. Returns True if it was present (and live), else False."""
        self._validate_key(key)
        now = self._clock.now()

        with self._lock():
            entry = self._entries.get(key)

            if entry is None or entry.is_expired(now):
                # WHY: deleting a missing OR already-expired key reports False —
                #      from the caller's view that key was not live, so there
                #      was nothing to delete. We still drop a found-expired key.
                self._entries.pop(key, None)
                return False

            del self._entries[key]
            return True

    def active_count(self) -> int:
        """Number of currently-live (non-expired) keys."""
        now = self._clock.now()

        with self._lock():
            # WHY: count by asking each Entry if it is alive at `now`. We do NOT
            #      trust the raw len(dict) because expired-but-not-yet-swept
            #      keys may still sit in the map. This is the count the spec
            #      calls "active" — live keys only.
            return sum(1 for entry in self._entries.values() if not entry.is_expired(now))

    def sweep_expired(self) -> int:
        """Reclaim every expired key right now. Returns how many were removed.

        A background reaper thread calls this on a timer so dead entries do not
        accumulate between reads. It is also safe to call by hand (tests do).
        """
        now = self._clock.now()

        with self._lock():
            # WHY: collect the dead keys FIRST, then delete — never mutate a
            #      dict while iterating it, that raises RuntimeError. One pass
            #      to find, one pass to remove.
            dead = [key for key, entry in self._entries.items() if entry.is_expired(now)]

            for key in dead:
                # WHY: pop(key, None), not del. Lazy expiry on a reader thread
                #      may have removed this same key a microsecond ago. pop is
                #      the safe-to-call-twice form, so the sweeper and a reader
                #      can both decide a key is dead without one of them
                #      crashing on a double-free.
                self._entries.pop(key, None)

            return len(dead)

    # ── validation helpers (typed errors only) ──────────────────────────────
    def _validate_key(self, key: str) -> None:
        # WHY: reject bad keys at the boundary, before touching the map. An
        #      empty string or a non-string is a caller bug — fail loud and
        #      typed, do not silently store under "".
        if not isinstance(key, str) or key == "":
            raise InvalidKeyError(f"key must be a non-empty string, got {key!r}")

    def _validate_ttl(self, ttl: float) -> None:
        # WHY: a ttl <= 0 means "already dead" — almost certainly a caller bug,
        #      so we reject it rather than storing a key that vanishes instantly.
        if not isinstance(ttl, (int, float)) or isinstance(ttl, bool) or ttl <= 0:
            raise InvalidTTLError(f"ttl must be a positive number of seconds, got {ttl!r}")


# ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# ThreadSafeTTLStore is the §9 "second version". It changes exactly one thing
# about the logic: _lock() now takes a real reentrant lock. It also runs a
# background sweeper thread so expired keys get reaped even with no reads. All
# of put/get/delete/active_count/sweep_expired are inherited UNCHANGED.
# 🎙️ "The thread-safe version overrides one method — _lock now grabs a real
#     RLock. RLock not Lock because a public method holding the lock may call
#     another guarded helper on the same thread; a plain Lock would deadlock on
#     the re-entry. I also start a daemon sweeper thread that calls the
#     inherited sweep_expired on a timer."
class ThreadSafeTTLStore(TTLStore):
    """Thread-safe store with a background expiry sweeper."""

    def __init__(self, clock: Clock | None = None, sweep_interval: float = 1.0) -> None:
        super().__init__(clock=clock)
        # WHY: RLock (reentrant) — the SAME thread can re-acquire it. If a future
        #      guarded method calls another guarded method, a plain Lock would
        #      deadlock; RLock lets the owning thread re-enter and only releases
        #      when the outermost `with` exits.
        self._mutex = threading.RLock()
        self._sweep_interval = sweep_interval
        self._stop = threading.Event()
        self._sweeper: threading.Thread | None = None

    # WHY: the ONLY logic override. Same shape as the no-op above, but now it
    #      actually guards the dict. Every public method already wraps its
    #      critical section in `with self._lock()`, so this single override
    #      makes all of them thread-safe — zero changes to the core file.
    @contextmanager
    def _lock(self) -> Iterator[None]:
        with self._mutex:
            yield

    def start_sweeper(self) -> None:
        """Launch the background reaper thread (idempotent)."""
        if self._sweeper is not None and self._sweeper.is_alive():
            # WHY: safe to call twice — second call does nothing. We do not want
            #      two sweeper threads racing the same map.
            return

        self._stop.clear()
        self._sweeper = threading.Thread(target=self._sweep_loop, daemon=True)
        # WHY: daemon=True so the reaper never blocks process shutdown.
        self._sweeper.start()

    def stop_sweeper(self) -> None:
        """Signal the sweeper to exit and wait for it (idempotent)."""
        self._stop.set()

        thread = self._sweeper

        if thread is not None:
            # WHY: join with a timeout so a wedged sweeper can never hang the
            #      caller forever. wait() inside the loop returns promptly once
            #      _stop is set, so this normally returns immediately.
            thread.join(timeout=self._sweep_interval + 1.0)
            self._sweeper = None

    def _sweep_loop(self) -> None:
        # WHY: Event.wait(timeout) is the sleep — but it wakes instantly when
        #      stop_sweeper sets the event, so shutdown is not delayed by a full
        #      interval. The loop exits as soon as _stop is set.
        while not self._stop.wait(self._sweep_interval):
            # WHY: reuse the inherited sweep_expired UNCHANGED. It already takes
            #      _lock() (now the real RLock), so the reap is atomic against
            #      put/get/delete. The sweeper adds no new logic, just a clock.
            self.sweep_expired()

# Python 3.11+
"""Narrated demo driver for the TTL key-value store.

Run me with:  python main.py
Each case prints "Case N ... -> OK" so a green run is obvious at a glance.
The FakeClock lets us "fast-forward time" without ever sleeping.
"""

from gateways import FakeClock
from models import InvalidKeyError, InvalidTTLError
from ttl_store_service import ThreadSafeTTLStore, TTLStore


def case_1_put_get_alive() -> None:
    clock = FakeClock(start=0.0)
    store = TTLStore(clock=clock)

    store.put("session", "alice", ttl=10)
    # WHY: at t=3 the key is well within its 10s TTL, so get returns the value.
    clock.advance(3)
    assert store.get("session") == "alice", "live key must return its value"
    assert store.active_count() == 1, "one live key expected"

    print("Case 1 put then get within TTL returns the value -> OK")


def case_2_lazy_expiry_on_read() -> None:
    clock = FakeClock(start=0.0)
    store = TTLStore(clock=clock)

    store.put("otp", "123456", ttl=5)
    # WHY: jump to exactly the deadline. is_expired uses >=, so the key dies AT
    #      t=5, not after — this proves the boundary, the classic off-by-one.
    clock.advance(5)
    assert store.get("otp") is None, "expired key must read as None"
    assert store.active_count() == 0, "expired key must not count as active"

    print("Case 2 key read at its exact deadline is expired and reclaimed -> OK")


def case_3_overwrite_resets_ttl() -> None:
    clock = FakeClock(start=0.0)
    store = TTLStore(clock=clock)

    store.put("k", "v1", ttl=10)
    clock.advance(8)
    # WHY: re-putting the same key replaces the value AND resets the TTL clock.
    #      So at t=8 we overwrite; the new key now lives until t=8+10=18.
    store.put("k", "v2", ttl=10)
    clock.advance(5)  # now t=13 — old TTL (t=10) passed, new TTL (t=18) not yet
    assert store.get("k") == "v2", "overwrite must reset the TTL and value"

    print("Case 3 re-putting a key resets its value and TTL -> OK")


def case_4_delete_and_invalid_inputs() -> None:
    clock = FakeClock(start=0.0)
    store = TTLStore(clock=clock)

    store.put("a", 1, ttl=10)
    assert store.delete("a") is True, "deleting a live key reports True"
    assert store.delete("a") is False, "deleting a missing key reports False"
    assert store.get("a") is None, "deleted key is gone"

    # WHY: every public boundary rejects bad input with a TYPED error, before
    #      mutating any state.
    for bad_key in ("", 123):
        try:
            store.put(bad_key, "x", ttl=5)  # type: ignore[arg-type]
            assert False, "bad key should have raised"
        except InvalidKeyError:
            pass

    for bad_ttl in (0, -3, "5"):
        try:
            store.put("ok", "x", ttl=bad_ttl)  # type: ignore[arg-type]
            assert False, "bad ttl should have raised"
        except InvalidTTLError:
            pass

    print("Case 4 delete is exact and bad key or ttl raises a typed error -> OK")


def case_5_active_sweeper_reaps_dead_keys() -> None:
    clock = FakeClock(start=0.0)
    store = ThreadSafeTTLStore(clock=clock, sweep_interval=0.02)

    store.put("short", "x", ttl=5)
    store.put("long", "y", ttl=100)

    # WHY: move the clock past the short key's deadline, then let the background
    #      sweeper thread wake and reap. We did NOT call get() on "short", so the
    #      ONLY way it leaves the map is the active sweeper — that is the point.
    clock.advance(10)
    store.start_sweeper()
    try:
        _wait_until(lambda: store.active_count() == 1, timeout=2.0)
    finally:
        store.stop_sweeper()

    assert store.get("short") is None, "sweeper must reap the expired key"
    assert store.get("long") == "y", "live key must survive the sweep"
    assert store.active_count() == 1, "exactly one live key remains"

    print("Case 5 background sweeper reaps expired keys, live keys survive -> OK")


def _wait_until(predicate, timeout: float) -> None:
    import time

    deadline = time.monotonic() + timeout

    while time.monotonic() < deadline:
        if predicate():
            return
        time.sleep(0.005)

    assert predicate(), "condition not met within timeout"


def main() -> None:
    case_1_put_get_alive()
    case_2_lazy_expiry_on_read()
    case_3_overwrite_resets_ttl()
    case_4_delete_and_invalid_inputs()
    case_5_active_sweeper_reaps_dead_keys()
    print("\nAll cases passed.")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

# Python 3.11+
"""Make the flat sibling modules importable from inside tests/.

The modules (models.py, gateways.py, ttl_store_service.py) live one level up.
Adding the parent directory to sys.path lets the test files do
`from models import Entry` exactly like main.py does.

import sys

sys.path.insert(0, os.path.dirname(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__))))

# Python 3.11+
"""Pytest suite for the TTL key-value store.

Covers every public method, every typed error path, the expiry boundary, the
lazy-vs-sweeper idempotency, and the headline concurrency test: writers racing
with readers and the active sweeper while the clock is advanced — with STRICT
post-state assertions that the active count is exactly right and no key is
double-freed or miscounted.
"""

import threading
import time
from contextlib import contextmanager

import pytest

from gateways import FakeClock
from models import Entry, InvalidKeyError, InvalidTTLError
from ttl_store_service import ThreadSafeTTLStore, TTLStore


@pytest.fixture
def clock() -> FakeClock:
    return FakeClock(start=0.0)


@pytest.fixture
def store(clock: FakeClock) -> TTLStore:
    return TTLStore(clock=clock)


# ── happy paths, one per public method ──────────────────────────────────────
def test_put_then_get_returns_value(store: TTLStore, clock: FakeClock) -> None:
    store.put("k", "v", ttl=10)
    clock.advance(9)
    assert store.get("k") == "v"


def test_get_missing_key_returns_none(store: TTLStore) -> None:
    assert store.get("nope") is None


def test_delete_live_key_returns_true(store: TTLStore) -> None:
    store.put("k", "v", ttl=10)
    assert store.delete("k") is True
    assert store.get("k") is None


def test_delete_missing_key_returns_false(store: TTLStore) -> None:
    assert store.delete("ghost") is False


def test_active_count_only_counts_live(store: TTLStore, clock: FakeClock) -> None:
    store.put("a", 1, ttl=5)
    store.put("b", 2, ttl=100)
    assert store.active_count() == 2
    clock.advance(5)  # "a" hits its deadline
    assert store.active_count() == 1


def test_overwrite_resets_value_and_ttl(store: TTLStore, clock: FakeClock) -> None:
    store.put("k", "v1", ttl=10)
    clock.advance(8)
    store.put("k", "v2", ttl=10)  # fresh 10s from t=8 -> dies at t=18
    clock.advance(5)  # t=13: old deadline (10) passed, new (18) not yet
    assert store.get("k") == "v2"


# ── expiry boundary (the off-by-one the interviewer probes) ─────────────────
@pytest.mark.parametrize(
    "elapsed, expected",
    [
        (4.0, "v"),   # before deadline -> alive
        (5.0, None),  # AT deadline -> dead (is_expired uses >=)
        (6.0, None),  # after deadline -> dead
    ],
)
def test_expiry_boundary(store: TTLStore, clock: FakeClock, elapsed, expected) -> None:
    store.put("k", "v", ttl=5)
    clock.advance(elapsed)
    assert store.get("k") == expected


def test_entry_is_expired_uses_inclusive_deadline() -> None:
    entry = Entry(value="v", expires_at=5.0)
    assert entry.is_expired(4.999) is False
    assert entry.is_expired(5.0) is True


# ── lazy expiry reclaims the slot ───────────────────────────────────────────
def test_get_reclaims_expired_key(store: TTLStore, clock: FakeClock) -> None:
    store.put("k", "v", ttl=5)
    clock.advance(5)
    assert store.get("k") is None
    # internal map no longer holds the dead key — lazy expiry popped it
    assert "k" not in store._entries


# ── typed error paths ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
@pytest.mark.parametrize("bad_key", ["", 0, None, 3.5])
def test_invalid_key_raises(store: TTLStore, bad_key) -> None:
    with pytest.raises(InvalidKeyError):
        store.put(bad_key, "v", ttl=5)


@pytest.mark.parametrize("bad_ttl", [0, -1, -0.5, "5", True])
def test_invalid_ttl_raises(store: TTLStore, bad_ttl) -> None:
    with pytest.raises(InvalidTTLError):
        store.put("k", "v", ttl=bad_ttl)


def test_get_validates_key(store: TTLStore) -> None:
    with pytest.raises(InvalidKeyError):
        store.get("")


def test_delete_validates_key(store: TTLStore) -> None:
    with pytest.raises(InvalidKeyError):
        store.delete("")


# ── sweeper + idempotency ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
def test_sweep_expired_removes_dead_returns_count(store: TTLStore, clock: FakeClock) -> None:
    store.put("a", 1, ttl=5)
    store.put("b", 2, ttl=5)
    store.put("c", 3, ttl=100)
    clock.advance(5)  # a and b dead, c alive
    removed = store.sweep_expired()
    assert removed == 2
    assert store.active_count() == 1
    # second sweep finds nothing — safe to call twice, no double-free error
    assert store.sweep_expired() == 0


def test_lazy_and_sweep_do_not_double_free(store: TTLStore, clock: FakeClock) -> None:
    # lazy expiry on get() pops the key, then the sweeper pops the same key —
    # the second pop must be a silent no-op, never a KeyError.
    store.put("k", "v", ttl=5)
    clock.advance(5)
    assert store.get("k") is None       # lazy expiry removes "k"
    assert store.sweep_expired() == 0   # sweeper finds nothing left, no crash


def test_background_sweeper_reaps_without_reads(clock: FakeClock) -> None:
    store = ThreadSafeTTLStore(clock=clock, sweep_interval=0.01)
    store.put("short", "x", ttl=5)
    store.put("long", "y", ttl=100)
    clock.advance(10)  # "short" now past its deadline

    store.start_sweeper()
    try:
        _wait_until(lambda: store.active_count() == 1, timeout=2.0)
    finally:
        store.stop_sweeper()

    assert store.get("short") is None
    assert store.get("long") == "y"


def test_start_sweeper_is_idempotent(clock: FakeClock) -> None:
    store = ThreadSafeTTLStore(clock=clock, sweep_interval=0.05)
    store.start_sweeper()
    first = store._sweeper
    store.start_sweeper()  # second call must not spawn a second thread
    try:
        assert store._sweeper is first
    finally:
        store.stop_sweeper()


# ── the lock is genuinely load-bearing (not a trivially-passing test) ───────
class _NoLockStore(ThreadSafeTTLStore):
    """Re-disables the guard so we can PROVE the lock is what prevents the race.

    This mimics the clean §6 core but kept on a background sweeper. Without the
    RLock, the sweeper's `for key, entry in self._entries.items()` runs at the
    same time a writer mutates the same dict -> CPython raises
    RuntimeError('dictionary changed size during iteration').
    """

    @contextmanager
    def _lock(self):
        yield


def _churn_vs_sweeper(store: ThreadSafeTTLStore, clock: FakeClock) -> list[BaseException]:
    # Pre-fill a LARGE population that STAYS large so the sweeper's iteration
    # over the dict takes long enough to be preempted mid-loop: many always-live
    # keys (the sweep must walk all of them every pass) plus a batch of dead
    # ones. Then churn the map from another thread the whole time. Without the
    # lock, the sweeper iterating while the churn thread inserts raises
    # RuntimeError('dictionary changed size during iteration').
    for i in range(5000):
        store.put(f"dead-{i}", i, ttl=1)
    for i in range(5000):
        store.put(f"live-{i}", i, ttl=1_000_000)
    clock.advance(5)  # the "dead-*" keys are now expired

    errors: list[BaseException] = []
    stop = threading.Event()

    # WHY: a failure inside the SWEEPER thread surfaces in that thread, not the
    #      churn thread. threading.excepthook catches uncaught exceptions from
    #      any thread so we can detect the race no matter where it fires.
    previous_hook = threading.excepthook

    def capture(args: threading.ExceptHookArgs) -> None:
        if args.exc_value is not None:
            errors.append(args.exc_value)

    threading.excepthook = capture

    def churn() -> None:
        i = 0
        while not stop.is_set():
            try:
                store.put(f"x-{i}", i, ttl=1_000_000)
                i += 1
            except BaseException as exc:  # noqa: BLE001 — capture race failure
                errors.append(exc)
                return

    try:
        store.start_sweeper()
        worker = threading.Thread(target=churn)
        worker.start()
        time.sleep(0.2)  # let the sweeper and churn overlap for many iterations
        stop.set()
        worker.join()
        store.stop_sweeper()
    finally:
        threading.excepthook = previous_hook

    return errors


def test_unlocked_sweeper_actually_races_and_raises() -> None:
    # Run a few trials — without the lock the iterate-vs-mutate race surfaces
    # reliably under sustained contention on a big map.
    raised = False

    for _ in range(8):
        c = FakeClock(0.0)
        store = _NoLockStore(clock=c, sweep_interval=0.0)
        if _churn_vs_sweeper(store, c):
            raised = True
            break

    assert raised, "expected the unlocked sweeper to hit the dict-mutation race"


def test_locked_sweeper_never_races() -> None:
    # The real thread-safe store under the SAME contention never raises — the
    # RLock makes the sweep's iteration atomic against concurrent puts.
    for _ in range(5):
        c = FakeClock(0.0)
        s = ThreadSafeTTLStore(clock=c, sweep_interval=0.0)
        errors = _churn_vs_sweeper(s, c)
        assert not errors, f"locked store must never race, got {errors}"


# ── the headline concurrency test ───────────────────────────────────────────
def test_writers_readers_and_sweeper_race_exact_active_count() -> None:
    # N writers each put a UNIQUE key. Half the keys get a SHORT ttl (will be
    # swept after we advance the clock), half get a LONG ttl (must survive).
    # Readers hammer get() at the same instant, and the active sweeper runs the
    # whole time. After advancing the clock past the short deadline we assert:
    #   - every long key still reads its exact value
    #   - every short key is gone from get()
    #   - active_count() is EXACTLY the number of long keys
    #   - the internal map holds no expired entry (no miscount, no double-free)
    clock = FakeClock(start=0.0)
    store = ThreadSafeTTLStore(clock=clock, sweep_interval=0.005)

    n_short = 60
    n_long = 40
    total = n_short + n_long

    short_keys = [f"short-{i}" for i in range(n_short)]
    long_keys = [f"long-{i}" for i in range(n_long)]

    barrier = threading.Barrier(total)
    errors: list[Exception] = []
    lock = threading.Lock()

    def writer(key: str, ttl: float) -> None:
        try:
            barrier.wait()  # all writers fire put() at the same instant
            # WHY: a tiny hold inside the window so writes genuinely overlap the
            #      sweeper and each other — proves the lock actually serialises
            #      the map mutation rather than the work being too fast to race.
            store.put(key, key, ttl=ttl)
            time.sleep(0.001)
        except Exception as exc:  # noqa: BLE001 — capture any thread failure
            with lock:
                errors.append(exc)

    threads = [
        threading.Thread(target=writer, args=(k, 5))
        for k in short_keys
    ] + [
        threading.Thread(target=writer, args=(k, 1000))
        for k in long_keys
    ]

    # sweeper runs concurrently with the writers from the very start
    store.start_sweeper()
    try:
        for t in threads:
            t.start()
        for t in threads:
            t.join()

        assert not errors, f"no writer should have raised, got {errors}"
        # all keys are in before any expiry — full population is live
        _wait_until(lambda: store.active_count() == total, timeout=2.0)

        # NOW advance the clock past the short deadline. Lazy readers AND the
        # background sweeper will both try to reclaim the short keys.
        clock.advance(10)

        # readers race the sweeper to reclaim the now-dead short keys
        read_results: dict[str, object | None] = {}
        read_barrier = threading.Barrier(len(short_keys) + len(long_keys))

        def reader(key: str) -> None:
            try:
                read_barrier.wait()
                value = store.get(key)
                with lock:
                    read_results[key] = value
            except Exception as exc:  # noqa: BLE001
                with lock:
                    errors.append(exc)

        readers = [threading.Thread(target=reader, args=(k,)) for k in short_keys + long_keys]
        for t in readers:
            t.start()
        for t in readers:
            t.join()

        assert not errors, f"no reader should have raised, got {errors}"

        # let the sweeper settle the final state
        _wait_until(lambda: store.active_count() == n_long, timeout=2.0)
    finally:
        store.stop_sweeper()

    # STRICT post-state assertions ------------------------------------------
    # 1. every short key reads None (expired, reclaimed by lazy OR sweeper)
    for k in short_keys:
        assert read_results[k] is None, f"{k} should have expired"
        assert store.get(k) is None, f"{k} must stay gone"

    # 2. every long key still has its EXACT value (no live key was reaped)
    for k in long_keys:
        assert store.get(k) == k, f"{k} must survive with its value"

    # 3. the active count is EXACTLY the number of long keys — not approx,
    #    not "at least". This is the invariant actually bound by the race.
    assert store.active_count() == n_long

    # 4. the internal map holds no expired entry — proves no miscount and that
    #    lazy expiry and the sweeper did not leave a dead key behind nor a live
    #    key short. Every remaining key is a long key, present exactly once.
    remaining = set(store._entries.keys())
    assert remaining == set(long_keys)
    assert len(remaining) == n_long


# ── helper ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
def _wait_until(predicate, timeout: float) -> None:
    deadline = time.monotonic() + timeout

    while time.monotonic() < deadline:
        if predicate():
            return
        time.sleep(0.002)

    assert predicate(), "condition not met within timeout"
